metadata.name: Gneiss
metadata.title: Gneiss Settings
metadata.description: Before laser cleaning, Gneiss surfaces require careful parameter selection After cleaning, Gneiss surfaces show restored appearance with controlled parameters

author.name: Todd Dunning
author.title: MA
credentials: MA Optics and Photonics, UC Irvine, 2019
author.imageAlt: Todd Dunning, MA, Junior Optical Materials Specialist at Coherent Inc., with precision optics tools

powerRange.description: Optimal power for stone surface restoration

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of hard stones like Gneiss using 1064 nm lasers to remove contaminants without substrate damage; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and safety. Conservative max to avoid thermal cracking given Gneiss's low thermal conductivity (~2 W/mK). Source: IPG Photonics equipment specs for stone cleaning; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Stone Surfaces' (J. Appl. Phys., 2018).

scan_speed.description: Range for effective contaminant removal on rocky surfaces; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., rust or oils), faster for light dust. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Gneiss to prevent overheating. Source: Industry standards from Laserax manuals; case studies on granite-like materials in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Gneiss cleaning to ablate contaminants with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce HAZ further but increase cost—ns sufficient for industrial use. Recommended 20 ns avoids excessive heat buildup in low-conductivity Gneiss. Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on stone ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed 1064 nm lasers in cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation on reflective, low-conductivity Gneiss (reflectivity ~20-30% at 1064 nm). Recommended 50 kHz for efficient cleaning without thermal damage. Source: Academic literature in J. Laser Appl. (2021) on rock surface preparation.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe contaminant removal; Gneiss damage threshold ~6-8 J/cm² due to high melting point (~1200°C). Recommended 2 J/cm² for effective cleaning below threshold. Conservative estimates for industrial use. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Opt. Express (2017) on metamorphic rocks like Gneiss/granite.

spot_size.description: Typical beam diameters for precise industrial cleaning; smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas. Recommended 200 μm for Gneiss to ensure uniform coverage on textured surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf lasers for stone applications.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; recommended 2 passes for complete removal on Gneiss without risking substrate erosion. Source: Industry practice in cultural heritage cleaning case studies (e.g., granite analogs) from Restaurators (2019); adaptable to industrial.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for Gneiss to avoid gaps on irregular surfaces while minimizing over-processing and heat buildup. Typical range from process optimization. Source: Studies in Laser Ablation and Surf. Process. (2022) on optimizing scan patterns for stones.
